Topic: If aggregate demand is demonstrably deficient and private investment shows no sign of spontaneous recovery, at what precise point does continued fiscal austerity become not a policy error but an act of deliberate economic self-destruction?
Austen — Judgement:
The question before me is fundamentally one of motive, not of macroeconomic calculation — for in my experience, the names we give our injuries rarely reveal what we truly intend by inflicting them.

When aggregate demand falters and no private confidence steps forward to restore it, the patient languishes in plain view; the physician who withholds remedy at this point is no longer treating the disease but demonstrating a philosophy. I have written at length about characters who mistake the *performance* of prudence for prudence itself — who deny themselves and others not from necessity, but from a deep satisfaction in the act of denial. Such austerity, dressed in the language of responsibility, is vanity; specifically, the vanity of those who are insulated from consequence and thus free to admire the architecture of their own restraint while others grow cold. The precise threshold arrives when the evidence of harm becomes undeniable and the policy continues without revision — that is not error; that is preference.

*Economy elevated to principle becomes the most expensive indulgence a governing class can afford.*
Hayek — Judgement:
The question accepts "aggregate demand" as a clinical diagnosis, but I have spent my career demonstrating that such macroeconomic aggregates are statistical fictions — they conceal the only thing that truly matters: the structure of relative prices and the coordination of knowledge dispersed among millions of minds no planner can reach.

What Keynesians call a demand shortfall is the market rendering its honest verdict on capital malinvested during a prior boom engineered by credit expansion. To respond with fiscal stimulus is not to cure the patient but to re-administer the very intoxicant that caused the illness; it perpetuates distortions in the production structure that must be liquidated before self-sustaining recovery becomes possible. The spontaneous order of the price system is the only discovery mechanism mankind possesses — suppress it with government expenditure and you guarantee a deeper, more protracted disorder.

The threshold you seek does not exist: austerity following a credit-driven boom is not self-destruction but the minimum cost of rebuilding a foundation on which genuine prosperity can grow.

*The self-destruction was consummated in the boom; what you condemn as austerity is merely the market's insistence on presenting the bill.*
Adam Smith — Judgement:
The question before us is not merely technical but moral: whether the discipline imposed upon public expenditure serves the genuine wealth of the nation or merely the composed interests of those who live by profits and rents.

I have long held that parsimony in private hands augments the stock available for productive employment — but public retrenchment, when private investment has already withdrawn, removes from circulation the very funds that would set industry in motion. The invisible hand presupposes hands that are actually working. No society can be flourishing, as I wrote plainly, of which the greater part of the members are poor and miserable. Those who counsel continued retrenchment under demonstrable deficiency wear the mask of prudence whilst serving creditors over labourers — a disposition I identified in the merchants of my own age as systematically corrupting to sound legislation.

Austerity becomes deliberate self-destruction the moment its architects can no longer plead ignorance of its effects upon those who live by wages.

*The parsimony of the sovereign that starves the labour it professes to protect is not frugality but a fraud upon the common weal.*
